238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
240	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
242	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
243	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
244	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
245	  exact sequence
246	  \[
247	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
248	  \]
249	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular the localised map \(i_f\) is injective and the
250	  localised map \(q_f\) is surjective. Here \(A_f = \texttt{Localization.Away}~f\)
251	  and \(M_f = \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers}~f)\,M\), the localisation
252	  taken at the submonoid of powers of \(f\) after restricting scalars to \(A\).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	
257	  Localisation of modules at a multiplicative set is an exact functor: it preserves
258	  injections, surjections, and exactness at the middle term. Applying it to the
259	  scalar-restricted maps \(i\) and \(q\) (regarded as \(A\)-linear via the tower
260	  \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence, exact in all three positions. In the
261	  formalisation this is the localisation-preserves-exactness API
262	  (\texttt{LocalizedModule.map\_exact}) applied to \(i\) and \(q\) regarded as
263	  \(A\)-linear maps via the scalar restriction along \(A \to B\).
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
267	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
268	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
270	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
271	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
272	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
273	\end{lemma}
274	
275	\begin{proof}
276	
277	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
278	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
279	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
280	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
281	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
282	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
283	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
284	\end{proof}

286	\begin{lemma}\leanok
287	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
288	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
289	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
290	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
291	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
292	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
293	\end{lemma}
294	
295	\begin{proof}
296	
297	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
298	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
299	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
300	\end{proof}

302	\begin{lemma}\leanok
303	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
304	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
305	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
306	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
307	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
308	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark
309	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1737-L1745).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
311	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
312	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
313	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
314	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
315	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form
316	  %  $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. This reduces us to the case where $B$
317	  %  is a domain and $M=B$."

319	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
320	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and

328	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).

332	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
333	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
334	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
335	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the

368	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).

372	  %  satisfied by a finite set of

374	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
375	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
376	  %   Let K := FractionRing A and A_g := Localization.Away g; the canonical map
377	  %   A_g -> K (induced because powers of g are non-zero-divisors of A) extends along
378	  %   MvPolynomial.map to MvPolynomial (Fin n) A_g -> MvPolynomial (Fin n) K.
379	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
380	  %       (p : MvPolynomial (Fin n) (FractionRing A)) :
381	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0),
382	  %         p ∈ Set.range
383	  %           (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)))
384	  % NOTE (iter-008 encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
385	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
386	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
387	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
388	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
389	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
390	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
391	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
392	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
393	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
394	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
395	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
396	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
397	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
398	\end{lemma}
399	
400	\begin{proof}
401	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
402	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
403	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
404	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
405	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
406	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
407	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
408	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
409	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
410	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
411	\end{proof}

481	\begin{proof}
482	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
483	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
484	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
485	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
486	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
487	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
488	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
489	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
490	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
491	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
492	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
493	
494	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
495	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
496	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
497	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
498	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
499	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
500	
501	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
502	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
503	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
504	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
505	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
506	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
507	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
508	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
509	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
510	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
511	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
512	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
513	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
514	
515	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
516	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending
517	  \(X_j \mapsto b_j\); the algebraic independence of the \(b_j\) over \(A_g\) (inherited
518	  from their independence over \(K\)) makes \(\varphi\) injective, and the
519	  module-finiteness just established is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over the
520	  image of \(\varphi\).
521	\end{proof}

523	\begin{lemma}
524	
525	\leanok
526	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
527	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
528	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
529	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
530	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
532	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
533	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
534	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
535	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
536	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
537	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
538	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
539	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
540	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
541	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
542	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
543	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
544	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
545	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
546	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
547	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
548	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
549	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
550	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
551	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
552	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
553	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
554	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
555	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
556	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
557	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
558	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
559	  \[
560	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
561	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
562	  \]
563	
564	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
565	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
566	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
567	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
568	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
569	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
570	\end{lemma}
571	
572	\begin{proof}
573	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
574	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
575	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
576	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
577	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
578	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
579	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
580	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
581	
582	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
583	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
584	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
585	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
586	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
587	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
588	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
589	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
590	
591	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
592	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
593	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
594	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
595	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
596	\end{proof}

641	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)

648	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
649	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
650	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
651	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors

656	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
657	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
658	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
659	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
660	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
661	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
662	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
663	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
664	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
665	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
666	\end{proof}

670	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}

674	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of

677	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
678	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
679	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
680	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
681	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
682	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
683	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
684	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
685	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
686	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
687	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
688	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
689	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
690	  %         IsUnit
691	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
692	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
693	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
694	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
695	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
696	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
697	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
698	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
699	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — viewed via \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\) as a univariate polynomial
700	  in \(X_0\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) — has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient (namely
701	  the image of \(g\)). Equivalently, \(e(F)\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over
702	  \(A_g\).
703	\end{lemma}
704	
705	\begin{proof}
706	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
707	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
708	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
709	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
710	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
711	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
712	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
713	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
714	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
715	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
716	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
717	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
718	\end{proof}

832	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),

835	  %   one may always take m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); the strong induction
836	  %   accepts any m' < d.
837	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
838	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
839	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
840	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
841	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
842	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
843	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
844	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
845	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
846	\end{lemma}

848	\begin{proof}
849	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
850	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
851	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
852	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
853	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
854	  is \(X_0\), the one eliminated by \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\); cf.\ the variable-convention
855	  note on \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
856	
857	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
858	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
859	
860	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
861	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
862	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
863	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
864	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
865	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
866	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
867	
868	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
869	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
870	  \(e(F)\) (whose \(\texttt{finSuccEquiv}\)-leading coefficient is a unit): the quotient
871	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
872	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
873	  \(A_g\).
874	
875	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
876	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
877	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
878	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
879	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[A square matrix is invertible iff its determinant is]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_isUnit_iff_isUnit_det}
84	  \lean{Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring, \(A\) is a unit of the
88	  matrix ring iff \(\det A\) is a unit of the scalar ring.
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Left Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
94	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Right Cramer inverse]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
104	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
108	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}
112	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
113	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
114	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
115	  \mathlibok
116	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
117	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
118	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
119	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
120	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
124	
125	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
126	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
127	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
128	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
129	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
130	
131	\begin{definition}
132	
133	\leanok
134	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
135	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
136	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
137	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
138	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
139	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
140	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
141	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
142	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
143	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
144	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
145	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
146	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With the chart ring
147	  %   `R^I := MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q : Fin r // q ∉ I}) ℤ`, define
148	  %   `universalMatrix d r I : Matrix (Fin d) (Fin r) R^I` whose entry at
149	  %   `(p, q)` is: the identity entry `if (orderIso I) p = q then 1 else 0`
150	  %   when `q ∈ I` (using the order isomorphism `Fin d ≃o I` from `#I = d`),
151	  %   and the indeterminate `MvPolynomial.X (p, ⟨q, hq⟩)` when `q ∉ I`.
152	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
153	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
154	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
155	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
156	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
157	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
158	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
159	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
160	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
161	\end{definition}

273	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
274	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
275	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
276	\end{definition}

278	\begin{lemma}
279	
280	\leanok
281	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
282	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
284	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
285	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). A conjunction (or pair of
286	  %   lemmas):
287	  %   `universalMinorInv d r I J * universalMinor d r I J = 1` and
288	  %   `universalMinor d r I J * universalMinorInv d r I J = 1`.
289	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
290	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
291	  genuine two-sided inverse:
292	  \[
293	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
294	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
295	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
296	  \]
297	\end{lemma}
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
300	  \leanok
301	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
302	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
303	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
304	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
305	\end{proof}

328	  \]

369	\begin{lemma}
370	
371	\leanok
372	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
373	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
374	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
375	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
376	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
377	  % affine patches" (read from
378	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
379	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
380	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
381	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
382	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
383	  %   `isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J :`
384	  %   `IsUnit (transitionPreMap d r I J (minorDet d r J I))`.
385	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
386	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
387	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
388	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
389	  \[
390	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
391	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
392	  \]
393	  which is a unit.
394	\end{lemma}
395	\begin{proof}
396	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
397	  \leanok
398	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
399	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
400	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
401	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
402	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
403	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
404	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
405	\end{proof}

407	\begin{definition}
408	
409	\leanok
410	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
411	  \label{def:gr_transition}
412	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
413	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
414	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
415	  % affine patches" (read from
416	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
417	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
418	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
419	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
420	  %  defined as follows. ...
421	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
422	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
423	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The away-localisation lift of the
424	  %   pre-hom along `P^J_I`:
425	  %   `transitionMap d r I J :`
426	  %   `Localization.Away (minorDet d r J I) →+* Localization.Away (minorDet d r I J)`
427	  %   `:= IsLocalization.Away.lift (minorDet d r J I)`
428	  %   `(isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet d r I J)` applied to
429	  %   `(transitionPreMap d r I J : R^J →+* R^I_J)`.
430	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
431	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
432	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
433	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
434	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
435	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
436	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
437	  \[
438	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
439	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
440	  \]
441	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
442	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
443	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
444	\end{definition}
445	
446	\begin{lemma}
447	
448	\leanok
449	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
450	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
451	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
452	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
453	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
454	  % affine patches" (read from
455	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
456	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
457	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
458	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target).
459	  %   `transitionMap_self d r I : transitionMap d r I I = RingHom.id _`
460	  %   (up to the identification `R^I_I = R^I` from `P^I_I = 1`).
461	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
462	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
463	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
464	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
465	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
466	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
467	\end{lemma}
468	\begin{proof}
469	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
470	  \leanok
471	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
472	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
473	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
474	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
475	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
476	\end{proof}
477	
478	\begin{lemma}
479	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
480	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
481	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
482	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self}
483	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
484	  % affine patches" (read from
485	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
486	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
487	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
488	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
489	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
490	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
491	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
492	  %  is satisfied."
493	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
494	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
495	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
496	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
497	  \[
498	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
499	  \]
500	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
501	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
502	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
503	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
504	  glue the charts.
505	\end{lemma}
506	
507	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
508	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
509	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
510	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
511	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
512	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
513	% L838-L848.)
514	\begin{proof}
515	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self}
516	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
517	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
518	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
519	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
520	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
521	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
522	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
523	
524	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
525	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
526	  \[
527	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
528	  \]
529	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
530	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
531	  \[
532	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
533	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
534	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
535	  \]
536	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
537	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
538	  \[
539	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
540	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
541	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
542	  \]
543	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
544	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
545	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
546	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
547	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
548	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
549	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
550	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
551	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
555	\label{sec:gr_glued}
556	
557	\begin{definition}
558	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
559	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
561	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
562	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
563	  % affine patches" (read from
564	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
565	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
566	  % "Therefore the schemes
567	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
568	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
569	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
570	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
571	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
572	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
573	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
574	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
575	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
576	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
577	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
578	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
579	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
580	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
581	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
582	  \[
583	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
584	  \]
585	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
586	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
587	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
588	
589	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
590	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
591	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
592	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
593	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
594	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
595	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
596	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
597	  follows.
598	\end{definition}
599	
600	\section{Separatedness}
601	\label{sec:gr_separated}
602	
603	\begin{lemma}
604	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
605	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
607	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
608	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
609	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
610	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
611	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
612	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
613	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
614	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
615	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
616	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
617	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
618	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
619	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
620	\end{lemma}
621	
622	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
623	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
624	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
625	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
626	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
627	\begin{proof}
628	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
629	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
630	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
631	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
632	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
633	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
634	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
635	  homomorphism is surjective.
636	
637	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
638	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
639	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
640	  \[
641	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
642	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
643	    \qquad
644	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
645	  \]
646	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
647	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
648	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
649	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
650	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
651	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
652	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
653	  and in particular
654	  \[
655	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
656	  \]
657	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
658	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
659	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
660	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
661	  \[
662	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
663	  \]
664	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
665	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
666	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
667	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
668	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
669	\end{proof}
670	
671	\section{Properness}
672	\label{sec:gr_proper}
673	
674	\begin{lemma}
675	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
676	  \label{lem:gr_proper}
677	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}
678	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated}
679	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
680	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891).
681	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
682	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
683	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
684	  %  Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
685	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
686	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
687	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
688	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
689	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
690	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
691	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
692	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
693	  %
694	  %  Now consider the homomorphism
695	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
696	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
697	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
698	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
699	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
700	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
701	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
702	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
703	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
704	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
705	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
706	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
707	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
708	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
709	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
710	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is proper.
711	\end{lemma}
712	
713	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
714	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
715	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
716	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
717	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
718	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
719	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
720	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
721	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
722	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
723	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
724	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
725	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
726	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
727	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
728	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
729	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
730	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
731	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
732	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
733	\begin{proof}
734	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
735	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
736	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
737	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
738	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
739	  \[
740	    \begin{array}{ccc}
741	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
742	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
743	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
744	    \end{array}
745	  \]
746	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
747	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
748	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
749	
750	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
751	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
752	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
753	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
754	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
755	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
756	  \(K\). Define
757	  \[
758	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
759	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
760	  \]
761	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
762	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
763	  landing in \(U^J\).
764	
765	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
766	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
767	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
768	  \[
769	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
770	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
771	    \qquad
772	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
773	  \]
774	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
775	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
776	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
777	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
778	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
779	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
780	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
781	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
782	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
783	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
784	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
785	
786	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
787	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
788	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
789	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
790	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
791	  proper.
792	\end{proof}
793	
794	\section{Out of scope}
795	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
796	
797	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
798	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
799	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
800	are not treated here:
801	\begin{itemize}
802	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
803	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
804	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
805	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
806	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
807	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
808	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
809	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
810	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
811	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
812	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
813	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
814	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
815	    that base change.
816	\end{itemize}
817

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}
182	
183	\begin{theorem}\leanok
184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]

244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).

255	  \leanok
256	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
257	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
258	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
259	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
260	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +

262	  % ``isAffineOpen_opensRange`` — a real proof against the gluing infrastructure,
263	  % NOT against a trivial identity placeholder. The Lean type remains the
264	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
265	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
279	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.

285	\begin{theorem}\leanok
286	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
287	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
289	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
290	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
291	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
292	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
293	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
294	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
295	  % is available.
296	  
297	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
298	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
300	  % "In Situation REF.
301	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
302	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
303	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
304	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
305	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
307	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]
311	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
312	  spectrum of the global sections.
313	\end{theorem}
314	
315	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write \(S = \Spec(R)\) and \(A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})\).
316	% Then \(A\) is an \(R\)-algebra and \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A\).
317	% The ring map \(R \to A\) gives rise to a canonical map
318	% \(\)
319	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
320	% \longrightarrow
321	% S = \Spec(R).
322	% \(\)
323	% We have
324	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
325	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
326	% Hence there is a canonical map
327	% \(\)
328	% \varphi_{univ} :
329	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
330	% \longrightarrow
331	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
332	% \(\)
333	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
334	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
335	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."
336	\begin{proof}
337	  
338	  \leanok
339	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
340	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
341	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
342	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
343	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than
344	  % the canonical iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X,𝒜))'' named below (iter-174+
345	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
349	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
350	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
352	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
357	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
359	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
360	  \(T \to \Spec A\) by the affine universal property
361	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
362	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
363	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
364	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
365	\end{proof}

367	\section{Lean encoding}
368	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
369	
370	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
371	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
372	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
373	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
374	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
375	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
376	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
377	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
378	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
379	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
380	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
381	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
382	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
383	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
384	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
385	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
386	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
387	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
388	infrastructure.
389	
390	\section{Out of scope}
391	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
392	
393	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core
394	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
395	are explicitly out of scope here:
396	\begin{itemize}
397	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)} (A.1.c) --- consumed-by, not
398	    defined-here; its blueprint home is the planned chapter
399	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex}.
400	  \item \textbf{Line-bundle pullback on a product \(C \times_k T\)} (A.1.b) ---
401	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).
402	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}
403	    --- downstream properties that depend on assumptions on \(\mathcal{A}\)
404	    (locally finite type, flat, etc.), not unconditional consequences of the construction.
405	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a
406	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.
407	\end{itemize}

12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
