24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE (iter-007): blocked on absent tensor/monoidal structure for SheafOfModules.
160	  % The degree-m component is Gamma(X_s, L_s^{otimes m}); Mathlib at the pinned commit has no
161	  % MonoidalCategoryStruct on X.Modules (synthesis fails) and no SheafOfModules/PresheafOfModules
162	  % tensor lemmas, so L^{otimes m} cannot even be named. This is a deep infrastructure prerequisite
163	  % (tensor product of sheaves of modules + lax-monoidal global sections), to be blueprinted as a
164	  % separate multi-lemma sub-build. See task_results .../QuotScheme.md.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}

200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}

237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}

307	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
308	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
310	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
311	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
312	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
313	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
315	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
316	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
317	  %  function
318	  %  $$
319	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
320	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
321	  %  $$
322	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
323	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
324	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
325	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Encode the graded ring as a graded
326	  %   `κ`-algebra `𝒜 : ℕ → Submodule κ A` with `[GradedAlgebra 𝒜]`, degree-0 part
327	  %   the scalars (`𝒜 0 = (1 : Submodule κ A)`, so `S₀ = κ` is a field), and the
328	  %   module as `ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M` with `[SetLike.GradedSMul 𝒜 ℳ]`
329	  %   (`[GradedModule 𝒜 ℳ]`). "Generated in degree 1" is
330	  %   `Algebra.adjoin κ (𝒜 1 : Set A) = ⊤`. The Hilbert function is
331	  %   `n ↦ Module.finrank κ (ℳ n)`. The conclusion is stated in the
332	  %   rational-series form CONSUMED by `Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly`
333	  %   (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib), i.e. via `PowerSeries.invOneSubPow`:
334	  %     {κ : Type*} [Field κ] {A : Type*} [CommRing A] [Algebra κ A]
335	  %       [IsNoetherianRing A] (𝒜 : ℕ → Submodule κ A) [GradedAlgebra 𝒜]
336	  %       (hgen : Algebra.adjoin κ (↑(𝒜 1) : Set A) = ⊤)
337	  %       {M : Type*} [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M]
338	  %       (ℳ : ℕ → Submodule κ M) [GradedModule 𝒜 ℳ]
339	  %       [∀ n, FiniteDimensional κ (ℳ n)] :
340	  %       ∃ (p : Polynomial ℚ) (d : ℕ) (N : ℕ), ∀ n > N,
341	  %         (Module.finrank κ (ℳ n) : ℚ)
342	  %           = (↑(Polynomial.toPowerSeries p) * ↑(PowerSeries.invOneSubPow ℚ d)
343	  %               : ℚ⟦X⟧).coeff n
344	  %   The exact graded-encoding typeclasses (`DirectSum.Decomposition`, the
345	  %   `S₀ = κ` identification, the `Polynomial.toPowerSeries`/unit coercions) are
346	  %   pinned by the scaffold pass; the load-bearing data are: finite-dimensionality
347	  %   of each `ℳ n` over `κ`, finite generation of `M`, degree-1 generation, and
348	  %   the `existsUnique_hilbertPoly`-compatible conclusion shape.
349	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
350	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
351	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
352	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
353	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
354	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
355	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
356	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
357	  such that for all \(n > N\)
358	  \[
359	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
360	  \]
361	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
362	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
363	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
364	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
365	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
366	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
367	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
368	
369	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
370	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
371	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
372	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
373	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
374	  rational series from the module.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
378	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
379	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
380	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
381	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
382	%  following short exact sequence
383	%  $$
384	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
385	%  $$
386	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
387	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
388	\begin{proof}
389	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
390	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
391	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
392	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
393	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
394	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
395	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
396	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
397	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
398	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
399	  graded modules
400	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
401	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
402	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
403	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
404	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
405	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
406	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
407	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
408	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
409	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
410	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
411	\end{proof}

413	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
414	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
416	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
417	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
418	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
419	  % Polynomials" (read from
420	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
422	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
423	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
424	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
425	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
426	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
427	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
428	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
429	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
430	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
431	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
432	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
433	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
434	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
435	  \[
436	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
437	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
438	  \]
439	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
440	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
441	\end{theorem}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
445	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
446	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
447	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
448	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
449	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
450	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
451	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
452	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
453	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
454	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
455	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
456	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
457	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
458	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
459	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
460	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
461	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
462	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
463	\end{proof}

465	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
466	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
467	
468	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
469	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
470	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
471	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
472	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
473	
474	\begin{definition}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
478	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
480	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
481	  % NOTE (iter-011): the DEFINITION itself is now formalized and axiom-clean (via the
482	  % always-available `ofIdeals` construction; the `ofIdeals` inclusion direction is the
483	  % landed lemma `Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le`). Only the FORWARD characterization
484	  % (the reverse inclusion identifying the annihilator with the module-annihilator on
485	  % affine opens) remains bridge-gated on lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen
486	  % (section restriction as a localization for quasi-coherent sheaves on a general scheme),
487	  % which is not yet formally closed.
488	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
489	  % Polynomials" (read from
490	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
491	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
492	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
493	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
494	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
495	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
496	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
497	  project-built primitive.}
498	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
499	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
500	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
501	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
502	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
503	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
504	  \(U \subseteq X\),
505	  \[
506	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
507	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
508	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
509	  \]
510	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
511	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
512	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
513	
514	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
515	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
516	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
517	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
518	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
519	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
520	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
521	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
522	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
523	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
524	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
525	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
526	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
527	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
528	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
529	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
530	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
531	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
532	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
533	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
534	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
535	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
536	
537	  \emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
538	  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
539	\end{definition}
540	
541	\begin{lemma}\leanok
542	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
543	  localization]
544	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
545	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
546	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
547	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
548	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
549	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
550	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
551	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
552	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
553	  \[
554	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
555	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
556	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
557	  \]
558	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
559	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
560	
561	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
562	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
563	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
564	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
565	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
566	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
567	\end{lemma}
568	
569	\begin{proof}
570	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
571	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
572	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
573	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
574	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
575	
576	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
577	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
578	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
579	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
580	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
581	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
582	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
583	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
584	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
585	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
586	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
587	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
588	\end{proof}
589	
590	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
591	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
592	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
593	  \mathlibok
594	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
595	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
596	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
597	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
598	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
599	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
600	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
601	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
602	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
603	\end{lemma}
604	
605	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
606	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
607	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
608	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
609	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
610	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
611	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
612	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
613	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
614	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
615	  %  associated to $M$.
616	  %  [...]
617	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
618	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
619	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). For a quasi-coherent sheaf of
620	  %   modules `F : X.Modules` (`SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent F`), an affine open
621	  %   `U : X.Opens` (`IsAffineOpen U`), and `f : Γ(X, U)` (the ring of sections,
622	  %   `X.presheaf.obj (op U)`), the section-restriction along the basic-open
623	  %   inclusion `D(f) = X.basicOpen f ⊆ U` exhibits `Γ(F, D(f))` as the
624	  %   localization of the `Γ(X, U)`-module `Γ(F, U)` at `Submonoid.powers f`.
625	  %   Write `ρ : Γ(F, U) →ₗ[Γ(X, U)] Γ(F, D(f))` for that restriction map (the
626	  %   additive `F.presheaf.map (homOfLE hD).op` made `Γ(X,U)`-linear by restriction
627	  %   of scalars along the ring map `Γ(X, U) → Γ(X, D(f))`). Then:
628	  %     {X : Scheme.{u}} (F : X.Modules) [SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent F]
629	  %       {U : X.Opens} (hU : IsAffineOpen U) (f : Γ(X, U)) :
630	  %       IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) ρ
631	  %   The companion ring statement `Γ(X, D(f)) = (Γ(X, U))_f`
632	  %   (`IsLocalization.Away f (Γ(X, D(f)))`) is lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib;
633	  %   the scalar tower `Γ(X, U) → Γ(X, D(f)) → Γ(F, D(f))` and the exact
634	  %   `Hom`/`presheaf.map` spelling of `ρ` are pinned by the scaffold pass.
635	  %   NOTE: only quasi-coherence is needed for this localization bridge — the
636	  %   finite-type hypothesis enters later (in def:modules_annihilator, via
637	  %   lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map), not here.
638	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
639	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
640	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
641	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
642	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
643	  \begin{enumerate}
644	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
645	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
646	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
647	    \item the section-restriction map
648	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
649	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
650	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
651	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
652	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
653	  \end{enumerate}
654	\end{lemma}
655	
656	\begin{proof}
657	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
658	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
659	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
660	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
661	
662	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
663	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
664	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
665	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
666	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
667	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
668	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
669	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
670	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
671	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
672	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
673	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
674	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
675	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
676	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
677	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
678	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
679	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
680	\end{proof}

688	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}

706	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
707	  \(X\).

710	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
711	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
712	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
713	  \mathlibok
714	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
715	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
716	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
717	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
718	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
719	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
720	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
721	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
722	\end{lemma}
723	
724	\begin{definition}
725	
726	\leanok
727	[Proper support over the base]
728	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
729	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
730	  \uses{def:schematic_support, lem:isProper_mathlib}
731	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
732	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
733	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
734	  % + L418-L419).
735	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
736	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
737	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
738	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
739	  %  [...]
740	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
741	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
742	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
743	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
744	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
745	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
746	  \[
747	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
748	  \]
749	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
750	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
751	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
752	  functor's pullback action requires.
753	\end{definition}

782	\label{sec:quot_functor}
783	
784	\begin{definition}\leanok
785	[Quot functor]
786	  \label{def:quot_functor}
787	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
788	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
789	
790	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
791	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
792	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
793	  % + L481-L498).
794	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
795	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
796	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
797	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
798	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
799	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
800	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
801	  %
802	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
803	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
804	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
805	  %
806	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
807	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
808	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
809	  %
810	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
811	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
812	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
813	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
814	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
815	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
816	  %  well-defined, which gives
817	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
818	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
819	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
820	  %  [...]
821	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
822	  %  as a co-product
823	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
824	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
825	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
826	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
827	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
828	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
829	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
830	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
831	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
832	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
833	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
834	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
835	  \[
836	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
837	  \]
838	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
839	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
840	  \begin{enumerate}
841	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
842	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
843	      which is flat over \(T\);
844	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
845	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
846	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
847	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
848	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
849	      \(\Phi\).
850	  \end{enumerate}
851	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
852	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
853	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
854	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
855	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
856	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
857	\end{definition}

859	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
860	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
861	
862	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
863	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
864	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
865	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
866	
867	\begin{definition}\leanok
868	[Grassmannian scheme]
869	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
871	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
872	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
873	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
874	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
875	  % + L773-L850).
876	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
877	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
878	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
879	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
880	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
881	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
882	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]

905	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
906	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
907	  \[
908	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
909	    \qquad
910	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
911	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
912	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
913	  \]
914	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
915	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
916	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
917	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
918	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).

989	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
990	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
991	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
992	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
993	  equipped with a tautological quotient
994	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
995	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
996	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
997	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
998	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
999	
1000	\end{theorem}

1002	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
1003	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
1004	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
1005	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
1006	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
1007	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
1008	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
1009	\begin{proof}
1010	  
1011	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
1012	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
1013	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
1014	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
1015	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
1016	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
1017	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
1018	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
1019	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
1020	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
1021	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
1022	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
1023	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
1024	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
1025	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
1026	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
1027	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
1028	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
1029	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
1030	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
1031	
1032	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
1033	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
1034	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
1035	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
1036	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
1037	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
1038	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
1039	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
1040	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
1041	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
1042	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
1043	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
1044	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
1045	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
1046	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
1047	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
1048	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
1049	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
1050	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
1051	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
1052	\end{proof}

1057	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
1058	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
1059	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
1060	\begin{itemize}

1064	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.

1066	    project-side substrate -- not constructed in this chapter.
1067	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
1068	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
1069	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is

1073	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;

1078	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
1079	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
1080	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
1081	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme

1084	    subproject.
1085	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
1086	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
1087	    constructed in this chapter.
1088	\end{itemize}

1183	[Codomain read of the section-level base-change map]
1184	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}

1189	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the

1191	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,

1197	  change'' (proof, ``\(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\)'' step).}
1198	  In the affine--affine model, with \(f' = \operatorname{pullback.snd}\) and
1199	  \(g' = \operatorname{pullback.fst}\) the legs of the pullback square, the global
1200	  sections of the codomain \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) are canonically
1201	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an \(R'\)-module. Explicitly, the assembled
1202	  isomorphism
1203	  \[
1204	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} :
1205	    \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)
1206	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
1207	  \]
1208	  is the composite of the pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
1209	  (for \((g')^*\)) followed by the pushforward dictionary
1210	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} (for \(f'_*\)).
1211	\end{lemma}
1212	
1213	\begin{proof}
1214	  \uses{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
1215	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1216	        \leanok
1217	  Here the two functors \((g')^*\) and \(f'_*\) are taken along the pullback cone legs,
1218	  which are \emph{not} a priori \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps; this is where
1219	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} enters. By that lemma
1220	  \(g' = \operatorname{pullback.fst}\) is, up to \texttt{pullbackSpecIso}, the
1221	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of the inclusion \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\), and
1222	  \(f' = \operatorname{pullback.snd}\) is the \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of
1223	  \(R' \to R' \otimes_R A\). With the legs so identified the affine dictionaries apply:
1224	  the pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} along
1225	  \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\) reads \((g')^*\widetilde M\) as the extension of scalars
1226	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and the pushforward dictionary
1227	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} along \(R' \to R' \otimes_R A\) reads
1228	  \(f'_*\) of it as restriction of scalars back to \(R'\). The result is
1229	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) as an \(R'\)-module, and composing the two dictionary
1230	  isomorphisms is \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). This matches the Stacks description
1231	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), with \(\mathcal{F}'\) the tilde of
1232	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).

1235	% --- Supporting infrastructure helpers for the codomain read (1-to-1 coverage) ---
1236	% These two file-internal helpers are what let the proof of
1237	% lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read pass the global sections of W₀ through the
1238	% pullbackSpecIso isomorphism: an iso f of schemes induces an equivalence of module
1239	% categories, whose adjunction unit is therefore an isomorphism.
1240	
1241	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1242	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1243	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1244	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1245	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1246	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1247	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1248	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1249	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1250	\end{lemma}
1251	\begin{proof}
1252	
1253	\leanok
1254	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1255	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1256	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1257	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1258	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1259	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1260	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1261	\end{proof}
1262	
1263	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1264	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1265	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1267	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1268	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1269	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1270	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1271	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1272	\end{lemma}
1273	\begin{proof}
1274	
1275	\leanok
1276	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1277	  \cref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1278	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1279	\end{proof}

1281	% --- Section-level identity, three pieces: regroup equiv (pure tensor algebra, native
1282	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the

1285	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1286	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1287	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1289	  % NOTE (iter-003): tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1290	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1291	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1292	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1293	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1294	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1295	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1296	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1297	  % L933--938).
1298	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1299	  % equality
1300	  % $$
1301	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1302	  % $$
1303	  % as $R'$-modules."
1304	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1305	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1306	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1307	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1308	  \[
1309	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1310	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1311	  \]
1312	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1313	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1314	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1315	  hypothesis.
1316	\end{lemma}
1317	
1318	\begin{proof}
1319	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1320	  \leanok
1321	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1322	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1323	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1324	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence
1325	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
1326	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). Crucially, that helper bundles
1327	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1328	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1329	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1330	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1331	  the exact \(\operatorname{extendScalars}/\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) packaging that
1332	  the codomain and domain reads produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1333	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1334	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1335	
1336	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1337	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1338	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1339	  (\(\operatorname{Algebra.TensorProduct.leftAlgebra}\), multiplication in the left
1340	  factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the dictionaries,
1341	  \((\operatorname{extendScalars}\iota)\,M\) with \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the
1342	  left inclusion, tensors over the \(\operatorname{restr}_\iota\) \(A\)-action arising
1343	  from \(\iota.\mathrm{toAlgebra}\). These two \(A\)-module structures on
1344	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1345	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1346	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1347	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1348	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1349	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1350	  \[
1351	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1352	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1353	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1354	  \]
1355	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1356	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1357	
1358	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1359	  promoting along \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\) yields the desired bundled
1360	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1361	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1362	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1363	\end{proof}

1367	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit of extension of scalars; reading it through the
1368	%     two tilde dictionaries pins it as the inverse of the regrouping isomorphism. ---

1370	\begin{lemma}
1371	
1372	\leanok
1373	[The section-level base-change map is the base change of the unit]
1374	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity}
1375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_section_identity}
1376	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1377	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1378	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1379	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1380	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1381	  % L932--937).
1382	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1383	  % equality
1384	  % $$
1385	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1386	  % $$
1387	  % as $R'$-modules."
1388	  % SOURCE (cohomological form): [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH
1389	  % `lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology', part (2) (read from
1390	  % references/stacks-coherent.tex, L967--968) — the H^0/direct-image-with-base-change
1391	  % specialisation this affine identity underlies.
1392	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cohomological form): "if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
1393	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$."
1394	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1395	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step); cohomological form Tag 02KH(2).}
1396	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
1397	  %   The conjugate of `Γ(θ)` through the domain read `Θ_src` and codomain read `Θ_tgt`
1398	  %   is the `R'`-base change of the algebraic unit `η_M : M → (A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M`,
1399	  %   `m ↦ (1 ⊗ₜ 1) ⊗ₜ m` (`LinearMap.lTensor R' η_M` post-composed with the `R'`-action),
1400	  %   which on the generator `r' ⊗ m` returns `r' • ((1 ⊗ 1) ⊗ m) = (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`; this is
1401	  %   exactly `(base_change_mate_regroupEquiv ψ φ M).inv`. The formalized conclusion is the
1402	  %   propositional equality of `ModuleCat R'` morphisms:
1403	  %     {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}} (ψ : R ⟶ R') (φ : R ⟶ A) (M : ModuleCat.{u} A) :
1404	  %       (base_change_mate_domain_read ψ φ M).inv ≫
1405	  %         (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).map
1406	  %           (pushforwardBaseChangeMap (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)
1407	  %             (Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))
1408	  %             (Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))
1409	  %             (IsPullback.of_hasPullback (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)).w (tilde M)) ≫
1410	  %         (base_change_mate_codomain_read ψ φ M).hom
1411	  %         = (base_change_mate_regroupEquiv ψ φ M).inv
1412	  In the affine--affine model (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\),
1413	  \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde M\) for an \(A\)-module
1414	  \(M\)), write \(\Gamma(\theta)\) for the section-level incarnation of the base-change
1415	  map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
1416	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}). Conjugate it through the two reads:
1417	  the domain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read})
1418	  identifies its source \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde M)\bigr)\) with \(R' \otimes_R M\),
1419	  and the codomain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
1420	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) identifies its target
1421	  \(\Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)\) with \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\). Then
1422	  the conjugated map
1423	  \[
1424	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\theta) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} :
1425	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M
1426	  \]
1427	  is the \(R'\)-base change of the algebraic unit of extension of scalars along
1428	  \(A \to A \otimes_R R'\), namely \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\),
1429	  \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\): explicitly it is the unique \(R'\)-linear map
1430	  sending \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \eta_M(m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
1431	  (i.e.\ \(\operatorname{LinearMap.lTensor} R'\,\eta_M\) followed by the \(R'\)-action on
1432	  the target). Equivalently, this conjugated map is the \emph{inverse} of the regrouping
1433	  isomorphism Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv},
1434	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma(\theta) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
1435	  = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\), and in particular an isomorphism with \emph{no}
1436	  flatness hypothesis.
1437	\end{lemma}
1438	
1439	\begin{proof}
1440	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
1441	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1442	  The argument is carried out \emph{directly on the module of global sections}; no
1443	  abstract adjoint-mate identification at the level of sheaves intervenes. By
1444	  hom-extensionality for \(R'\)-linear maps it suffices to check the asserted equality on
1445	  the \(R'\)-module generators \(r' \otimes m\) of the source \(R' \otimes_R M\), and by
1446	  \(R'\)-linearity on the generators \(1 \otimes m\).
1447	
1448	  \emph{The base-change map is the base change of the unit.} For a quasi-coherent sheaf
1449	  on an affine scheme the base-change comparison \(\theta\) is, by construction, the
1450	  pullback–pushforward comparison obtained by applying \(g^*\) to the unit of the
1451	  \(\bigl((g')^*, (g')_*\bigr)\)-adjunction; read on global sections through the two tilde
1452	  dictionaries this is precisely the extension of scalars along \(\psi : R \to R'\) of the
1453	  algebraic unit of extension of scalars along \(A \to A \otimes_R R'\). Concretely, the
1454	  unit \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\) sends \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1)
1455	  \otimes m\) (the codomain read identifies \(\Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)\)
1456	  with \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}),
1457	  and the domain read identifies the source with \(R' \otimes_R M\)
1458	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}). The base-change map therefore is the
1459	  \(R'\)-linearization \(\operatorname{LinearMap.lTensor} R'\,\eta_M\) of \(\eta_M\)
1460	  evaluated through the \(R'\)-action on the target: on the generator \(r' \otimes m\),
1461	  \[
1462	    r' \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \cdot \eta_M(m)
1463	    \;=\; r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
1464	    \;=\; (1 \otimes r') \otimes m ,
1465	  \]
1466	  using that the \(R'\)-action on \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\) is restriction of
1467	  scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\), \(r' \mapsto 1 \otimes r'\).
1468	
1469	  \emph{Identification with \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\).} The regrouping isomorphism
1470	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} has inverse sending
1471	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\) on generators. This is the same
1472	  assignment as the displayed map above, and both maps are \(R'\)-linear, so they
1473	  coincide. This is exactly the ``boils down to the equality''
1474	  \((A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M\) of the source, read as an identity of
1475	  \(R'\)-modules; the regrouping isomorphism realizes the equality and the base-change map
1476	  is its inverse. In particular the conjugated map is an isomorphism, with no flatness
1477	  hypothesis used anywhere.
1478	\end{proof}

1743	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.

1745	  \medskip

1747	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to

1787	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
1788	
1789	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
1790	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
1791	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

1793	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
1794	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1795	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
1796	  \mathlibok
1797	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1798	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
1799	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
1800	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
1801	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
1802	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
1803	  \[
1804	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
1805	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1806	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
1807	  \]
1808	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
1809	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
1810	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
1811	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
1812	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
1813	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
1814	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
1815	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
1816	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
1817	\end{lemma}

1850	  \(i = 0\) case.}
1851	  Consider the cartesian square
1852	  \[
1853	    \begin{array}{ccc}
1854	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
1855	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
1856	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S

1860	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
1861	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
1862	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
1863	  \[
1864	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1865	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .

1868	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map

1874	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of

1903	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
1904	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1905	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote

1910	% $$

1912	% is an isomorphism.
1913	%
1914	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
1915	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that

1917	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
1918	% $$
