1923	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
1924	% $$
1925	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
1926	% $$
1927	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
1928	% $$
1929	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
1930	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
1931	% $$
1932	% as follows from
1933	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
1934	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology.
1935	%
1936	% In case $X$ is quasi-separated, choose an affine open covering
1937	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
1938	% {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
1939	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
1940	% The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
1941	% can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
1942	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
1943	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
1944	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
1945	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
1946	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
1947	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
1948	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
1949	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
1950	\begin{proof}
1951	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
1952	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
1953	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
1954	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
1955	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
1956	  sequence.
1957	
1958	  \medskip
1959	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
1960	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
1961	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
1962	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1963	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
1964	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
1965	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1966	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
1967	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
1968	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
1969	  \[
1970	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1971	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1972	  \]
1973	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
1974	
1975	  \medskip
1976	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
1977	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
1978	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
1979	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
1980	  \[
1981	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
1982	    \;=\;
1983	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
1984	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
1985	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
1986	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
1987	    \Bigr),
1988	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
1989	  \]
1990	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
1991	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
1992	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
1993	  higher cohomology.
1994	
1995	  \medskip
1996	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
1997	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
1998	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
1999	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2000	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2001	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2002	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2003	  \[
2004	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2005	    \qquad
2006	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2007	  \]
2008	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2009	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2010	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2011	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2012	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, i.e.\ Mathlib's
2013	  \texttt{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}):
2014	  \[
2015	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2016	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2017	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2018	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2019	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2020	  \]
2021	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2022	
2023	  \medskip
2024	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2025	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2026	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2027	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2028	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2029	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2030	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2031	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2032	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2033	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2034	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2035	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2036	  \[
2037	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2038	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2039	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2040	  \]
2041	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2042	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2043	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2044	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2045	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2046	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2047	\end{proof}

904	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
905	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
906	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
907	  core of the induction).}
908	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
909	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
910	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
911	
912	  This is the genuine Mathlib-absent residue of generic freeness: the bottom of the
913	  Nitsure \S4 induction, on which the whole finite-type case rests.
914	\end{lemma}
915	
916	\begin{proof}
917	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
918	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
919	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
920	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
921	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
922	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE (iter-007): the induction must generalise the base domain A
923	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
924	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
925	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
926	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
927	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
928	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
929	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
930	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
931	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
932	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
933	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
934	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
935	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
936	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
937	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
938	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
939	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
940	  corresponding polynomial ring.
941	
942	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
943	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
944	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
945	
946	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
947	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
948	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
949	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
950	  \begin{enumerate}
951	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
952	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
953	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
954	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
955	      \(P_d\)-modules
956	      \[
957	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
958	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
959	      \]
960	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
961	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
962	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
963	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
964	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
965	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
966	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
967	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
968	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
969	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
970	      to \(A_g\) (see the structure note above).
971	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Writing \(h = a / g^k\) with
972	      \(a \in A\), the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is isomorphic to \(A_{g a}\) and
973	      \((T_g)_h\) to \(T_{g a}\); thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over \((A_g)_h\) transports
974	      to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the localisation-tower free-transport
975	      fact \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}. Set \(f := g a \ne 0\); then
976	      \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
977	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
978	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
979	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
980	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
981	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
982	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
983	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
984	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
985	      completes the induction.
986	  \end{enumerate}
987	\end{proof}

989	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
990	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
991	
992	The following two file-internal helpers in the \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace package
993	the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms (flatness and module-finiteness) that the
994	chain and the geometric globalization consume. Both are already proved axiom-clean.
995	
996	\begin{lemma}
997	
998	
999	\leanok
1000	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1001	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1002	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1003	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1004	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1005	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is
1006	  \emph{flat} over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
1007	\end{lemma}
1008	\begin{proof}
1009	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1010	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} the localized module is free over
1011	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}; free modules are flat (\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}).
1012	\end{proof}
1013	
1014	\begin{lemma}
1015	
1016	
1017	\leanok
1018	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1019	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1021	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1022	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra
1023	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite A B]}), and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module
1024	  (\texttt{[Module.Finite B M]}) with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the
1025	  scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such
1026	  that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is free over
1027	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
1028	\end{lemma}
1029	\begin{proof}
1030	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1031	  The \(A\)-finiteness of \(M\) is \emph{derived}, not assumed: since \(B\) is
1032	  module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\), composition along
1033	  the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\) makes \(M\) module-finite over \(A\) (transitivity
1034	  of module-finiteness, \texttt{Module.Finite.trans}). With \(M\) now a finite
1035	  \(A\)-module, \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} produces the required \(f \ne 0\) with
1036	  \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} free over
1037	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}. The lemma is therefore strictly more general than a
1038	  repackaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: the finite \(A\)-algebra \(B\) and the
1039	  finite \(B\)-module \(M\) are the genuine inputs, and \(A\)-finiteness is a
1040	  consequence of the hypotheses rather than one of them.
1041	\end{proof}

1048	[Generic flatness, geometric form]

1075	
1076	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1077	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1078	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1079	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1080	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1081	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty

1084	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1085	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1086	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1087	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1088	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1089	
1090	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
1091	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
1092	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
1093	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
1094	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
1095	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
1096	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
1097	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
1098	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
1099	\end{theorem}

1101	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1102	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1103	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1104	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1105	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1106	% by restricting to a non-empty
1107	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  
1110	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1111	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
1112	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
1113	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1114	
1115	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1116	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1117	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1118	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1119	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1120	
1121	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1122	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
1123	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
1124	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
1125	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
1126	  to the algebraic form.
1127	
1128	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1129	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1130	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1131	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1132	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
1133	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
1134	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
1135	
1136	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
1137	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
1138	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
1139	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
1140	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
1141	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
1142	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1143	\end{proof}
1144	
1145	\section{Mathlib status}
1146	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1147	
1148	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1149	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1150	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1151	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1152	generic-flatness argument consumes
1153	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1154	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1155	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1156	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1157	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1158	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1159	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1160	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1161	are introduced in
1162	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
1163	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
1164	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.

import json
with open('/home/archon/proj/Quot-Foundations/.leandag/dag.json') as f:
    dag = json.load(f)

nodes = dag['nodes']
edges = dag['edges']

# Build connectivity sets
from_set = set(e['from'] for e in edges)
to_set = set(e['to'] for e in edges)
connected = from_set | to_set

# Isolated = no edges at all
isolated = [n for n in nodes if n['id'] not in connected]
print('=== ISOLATED NODES ===')
for n in isolated:
    print(f'  {n[\"id\"]}: type={n[\"type\"]}, proved={n[\"proved\"]}, lean={n.get(\"lean_name\",\"\")}')

print()
# Sorry nodes
sorry_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n.get('has_sorry')]
print(f'=== WITH SORRY ({len(sorry_nodes)}) ===')
for n in sorry_nodes:
    print(f'  {n[\"id\"]}: lean={n.get(\"lean_name\",\"\")}')
"

## 7. Unstarted-phase proposals

### UP-1: `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent` QCoh bridge sub-build

**Status:** No blueprint coverage. No LEAN SIGNATURE. Referenced in STRATEGY.md as a "sizable new sub-build" and "Mathlib-gradient lane."

**Purpose:** Provides the QCoh→`IsLocalizedModule` bridge `lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen` (`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen`). This unlocks the forward annihilator characterization `(annihilator F).ideal U = Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U)`, which in turn feeds the rank-`r` local-freeness predicate (QUOT-defs P2).

**Mathematical content:** For an affine scheme `X = Spec A`, a basic open `D(f)`, and a quasi-coherent sheaf `F`, the section module `Γ(F, D(f))` is the localization of `Γ(F, X)` at `f`. Equivalently: `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isQuasicoherent` states that the canonical map `tilde(Γ(F,X)) → F` is an isomorphism when `F` is quasi-coherent. Mathlib provides only the global-presentation form; the local `IsLocalizedModule` form requires a sub-build.

**Trigger:** Open this lane when QUOT-repr approaches (GR-cells/glue/quot phases begin) OR when the forward annihilator characterization is needed for the rank predicate. Not blocking any current-iter prover.

**Recommendation:** When the writer opens this lane, add a new blueprint section to `Picard_QuotScheme.tex` (or a dedicated `Picard_QCohBridge.tex`) with the sub-build's lemmas and `% LEAN SIGNATURE` pins before dispatching a prover.

**Estimated effort:** 3–5 iters, 80–200 LOC.

---

The induction structure (generalizing `A` into the motive — `Nat.strong_induction_on d` with `A` reverted) is fully documented at lines 916–936.
